tribe_description:
This tribe shops the way a practical investigator makes decisions: they are trying to eliminate regret, not chase upside. A product is only “good” if it behaves predictably in their real routine—when they’re tired, rushed, traveling, or using it in less-than-ideal conditions (cramped space, low patience, dim light, no reading glasses). They assume the true cost of ownership is the nuisance tax: extra steps, unclear controls, features that chatter or nag, settings that don’t stay put, and any purchase that quietly forces more shopping just to reach day‑one usability. So they don’t get pulled into spec-sheet theater; they judge whether the core job stays simple under normal human use.

They grant trust through proof they can reproduce at home. Marketing claims are treated as hypotheses, and credibility is earned by agreement with a household reference they already believe—another device, a familiar baseline, or a routine benchmark they can rerun without special tools. When numbers or outcomes don’t line up, they don’t accept “best practices” as an excuse or soothe the mismatch with vibes; they retest, check defaults, and then state which device seems more believable and why. For livability claims (like noise, speed, convenience), they don’t rely on abstract micro-metrics; they anchor praise in concrete comparisons that readers can picture, because transferability matters more than technical-sounding detail.

Fairness is part of performance in their worldview. They perform an in-the-box inventory and treat “ready to operate immediately” as a value verdict, not a footnote—power/batteries, spares, and storage/case signal whether the purchase is complete or whether the brand is shifting hassle onto the buyer. They extend the same fairness audit to risk terms: explicitly stated warranty length and a clear return window are read as brand honesty and respect for the buyer’s time. Vague policy language is treated as hidden downside, and no amount of routine-care advice substitutes for clear terms.

In reviews, they write as consumer-protection and decision closure. They narrate a keep/return path: (1) does the core function match baseline reality, (2) is it livable day-to-day (readability, noise, speed, packability, state retention), and (3) is the decision closed—complete enough and transparent enough that you can stop shopping. When a product passes their threshold, they don’t invent a token “only ding” that would reopen the search; they explain the minimal “sanity setup” (what to disable, which settings matter) that makes it quiet, stable infrastructure. When something threatens closure—missing essentials, inconsistent outputs, or unclear warranty/returns—they elevate it as a deal-breaker because those are the traps that create repeat purchases, ongoing friction, and buyer regret.
tribe_definition: A tribe of analytical supplement sleuths who investigate products closely before sharing opinions. They generally prioritize careful analysis, supplements and wellness products.
population_definition: Health-conscious consumers who evaluate supplements, personal care, and wellness products.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating a purchase with performance claims, they treat the claims as hypotheses to be checked with evidence they can personally verify at home. They look for concrete, repeatable signals—stable outputs, explicit documentation, and simple checks they can redo on a normal day—before they’ll speak with confidence. If the support for a claim is vague, they keep enthusiasm separate from endorsement and frame the product as “maybe fine, but not proven enough to trust.”
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: They operationalize being “informed” as home-repeatable verification; this window’s gaps continued to show predictions drifting into generic usage talk while their actual logic hinged on whether claims could be personally checked (MEDIUM, recurring missing nuance).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When trying a product for themselves, they pressure-test it in realistic conditions and treat day-to-day livability as part of the result, not a separate topic. They report what changed in their routine, what got easier, and what became a recurring annoyance—especially under imperfect use (rushed mornings, travel, low patience). If the product only succeeds in a “perfect user” scenario, they frame that as functional failure rather than user error.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: They treat normal-life constraints as the true test environment; gaps repeatedly showed actual reviews centered on livability benchmarks while predictions diluted these into generic satisfaction (HIGH, multiple gap blocks).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When recommending a product, they disclose pricing context and try to be fair about what influenced their judgment (sales, bundles, and competing options they considered). They don’t treat “getting a deal” as the whole story; they use deal context to explain whether the product still makes sense at typical pricing. If they suspect their own judgment could be skewed by a discount or prior familiarity, they flag it so readers can recalibrate.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This remains a plausible continuity norm but was not directly strengthened by the current gap blocks (LOW-to-MEDIUM continuity).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When judging value, they translate price into total cost-of-use: what it saves in hassle and time, plus what it will quietly demand later (replacement cadence, accessories, or add-ons needed to operate normally). They’ll do explicit math when it fits, but the bigger driver is whether the product avoids recurring friction and follow-on purchases. Something can be inexpensive and still get called poor value if it creates ongoing “small taxes” on attention or forces more shopping to complete the job.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps reinforced that their value logic is total cost-of-use and decision closure: they penalize hidden follow-up purchases and recurring hassle as decisive value failures (HIGH, multiple gap blocks around completeness and stop-shopping).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating products with safety, durability, accuracy, or return risk, they look for explicit credibility signals as permission to trust the basics (clear sourcing/testing statements, certifications, and plainly stated policies). They get skeptical when transparency is implied rather than stated, and they treat missing disclosures as a reason to withhold endorsement even if the product seems pleasant to use. For them, trust is earned through concrete disclosure, not reassuring tone or maintenance advice.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This window strengthened the transparency norm: gaps showed predictions ignoring explicitly disclosed warranty/return terms and substituting care instructions, while the tribe treats stated terms as the trust proof itself (MEDIUM-HIGH, multiple gap blocks).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When writing a review, they structure it to help someone else make (or avoid) the purchase with minimal extra research. They lead with the baseline they compared against, what they tested in everyday use, and what would make them keep or return it. They include only the operational specifics that change outcomes—settings that matter, annoyances to disable—because they assume the reader wants a predictable decision, not a feature tour.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed they don’t pad reviews with generic tips; they foreground concrete decision points—baseline comparisons, livability, what arrived, and whether terms were explicit—to prevent hidden costs and regret (MEDIUM-HIGH).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When choosing products for routines, they optimize for cognitive-load reduction: fewer steps, fewer resets, fewer exceptions, and fewer things to remember. They reward tools that stay boringly consistent and penalize anything that introduces recurring decisions or maintenance overhead. Their satisfaction is tied to whether the habit survives long-term, not whether the first day felt exciting.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gap evidence continued to show friction reduction as a core success metric; they treat “good enough + stable + complete” as the win state, not an endless ledger of minor issues (MEDIUM, consistent window signals).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating device-like products, they treat setup friction and interface clarity as first-class performance criteria. They explicitly test whether core functions can be operated quickly and readably in normal conditions (including without reading glasses), and they downgrade products that make basic use feel like a procedure. In reviews, they name exactly where the UI slows them down because they believe friction predicts abandonment more reliably than advertised capability.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps consistently showed actual reviews foregrounding operability under normal conditions while predictions assumed “intuitive” use or added speculative time-per-use metrics; for this tribe, usability is part of the core function (HIGH, repeated across the window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When deciding whether to trust a product’s output, they benchmark it against a known household reference device they already rely on and report the agreement or drift in plain terms. They treat cross-device consistency as more persuasive than protocol-heavy advice because it’s the kind of proof other buyers can reproduce. If results don’t line up, they describe what they retested and then state which device they trust more and why, instead of deferring to ‘best practices’ rhetoric.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps repeatedly indicated that cross-device agreement is their trust bridge; predictions drifted into generic micro-metrics rather than the tribe’s baseline-first verification move (HIGH, repeated in the window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product advertises optional features, they audit whether those extras create noise, confusion, or repeated prompts that interfere with the core job. They do not treat feature count as automatic value; they evaluate whether each extra is understandable, non-intrusive, and—most importantly—cleanly controllable. Their praise often includes a ‘sanity setup’: what to disable or ignore so the product becomes quiet, simple infrastructure.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gap evidence showed predictions treating extras as pure positives, while actual review logic treated them as potential friction unless they can be disabled cleanly (HIGH, multiple related gap points).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When judging value, they perform an in-the-box inventory and treat practical completeness as a fairness test, not a minor detail. They enumerate whether essentials and support items are included (power/batteries, spares, storage/case) and fold that directly into the value verdict because missing basics create forced follow-on purchases. They extend the same completeness audit to risk terms: clearly stated warranty length and return window count as part of what you ‘get’ for the money.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed them treating included essentials and explicit warranty/return terms as decisive fairness/value proof; predictions minimized these and even fabricated a missing-essentials annoyance, contradicting the tribe’s inventory-based audit (HIGH, multiple gap blocks).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When describing day-to-day livability, they validate claims with relatable household baselines rather than speculative micro-metrics. They compare noise, speed, and convenience against familiar reference points readers can picture, because those benchmarks predict whether the product will actually get used. If a reviewer can’t anchor livability in something concrete, they consider the praise less transferable to other buyers.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed the tribe using concrete household comparisons (especially noise) as the reassurance move, while predictions softened those into generic adjectives or invented micro-timings (HIGH, multiple gap blocks).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When assessing reliability, they check for persistence and state retention—whether settings stick and the product doesn’t demand reprogramming after power changes. They treat forgetting as a recurring time leak that compounds into dislike even if performance is otherwise fine. Reviews call this behavior out directly because they see it as the difference between a tool and a repeating chore.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This window continued to emphasize recurring hassle as a real reliability cost; persistence remains a surfaced heuristic because it predicts ongoing friction more than one-time setup pain (MEDIUM-HIGH).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When they reach a ‘keeper’ verdict, they treat the review as a closure argument: the product is complete enough, stable enough, and transparent enough to stop shopping. They avoid manufacturing small negatives that would reopen the decision, and they instead clarify which imperfections don’t matter to the keep/return threshold. If something truly threatens closure (missing essentials, unclear warranty/returns, inconsistent output), they elevate it immediately as a deal-breaker rather than a casual ding.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed predictions injecting arbitrary ‘only ding’ complaints that contradicted the reviewer’s closed-decision logic; for this tribe, closure is a coherent threshold and reviews reflect it (MEDIUM-HIGH, repeated within the window’s error pattern).

main.text: When they spend money on an upgrade, they want gains to be noticeable, repeatable, and attributable to the product rather than wishful thinking. They are motivated by outcomes they can track in ordinary life—better consistency, fewer interruptions, or more dependable results—not abstract performance theater. This is why they compare against baselines and describe changes in practical, comparable terms.
main.rationalization: This motivation remains consistent with the window’s emphasis on baseline comparison and avoidance of speculative micro-metrics as the route to “real” gains (MEDIUM continuity).

main.text: When a product carries uncertainty (accuracy, durability, safety, or return risk), they are motivated to reduce that uncertainty quickly before they let it into a routine. They prefer trust that maps to everyday reality—agreement with a known reference, predictable settings, and explicit policies—over reassurance or technique-heavy advice. If certainty can’t be achieved without ongoing effort, they would rather simplify, return, or keep shopping than gamble.
main.rationalization: Gaps emphasized their trust path: baseline checks plus explicit warranty/return transparency, not substituted maintenance narration (HIGH, repeated window emphasis).

main.text: When buying anything that touches their body or meaningfully affects wellbeing, they are motivated by risk avoidance and interpret missing transparency as a hazard signal. They want brands to make purity/allergen/ingredient decisions easy by being explicit, because they assume hidden details become buyer problems later. This posture generalizes: unclear warranty/return terms are treated as another form of risk being shifted onto the customer.
main.rationalization: This window reinforced the broader rule that missing transparency (here, warranty/return clarity) is treated as risk-shifting, even when ingestible-specific evidence was not central (MEDIUM continuity).

main.text: When judging whether something is ‘worth it,’ they are motivated by a fairness-based value ethic: the product should respect their time and not require extra purchases or repeated hassle to deliver its promise. They don’t just want a low price; they want a ready-to-use experience with the downside made explicit up front. This is why they inventory box contents and react strongly to missing essentials or vague policies.
main.rationalization: Gaps highlighted that they fold in-the-box completeness and policy clarity into fairness judgments and reject value arguments that ignore hidden follow-on costs (HIGH, multiple gap blocks).

main.text: When they leave a review, they are motivated to prevent other buyers from paying for paper-perfect products that irritate in daily use. They see reviewing as consumer protection: translating small annoyances, confusing extras, missing essentials, and policy vagueness into clear keep/return guidance. This drives them to publish the few details that usually only become obvious after checkout and first-week use.
main.rationalization: Gaps showed them turning overlooked details (feature annoyance, completeness, warranty/return visibility) into buyer-protection evidence rather than generic commentary (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When building routines, they are motivated by simplification: fewer products, fewer settings, fewer steps, and fewer things to remember. Convenience is not laziness for them—it’s reliability, because a simpler routine is more likely to be followed consistently. This is why they reward state retention and controllable feature sets that let the core workflow stay quiet and stable.
main.rationalization: This window continued to tie satisfaction to controllability and low-cognitive-load operation; gaps around disabling features and avoiding recurring setup support this motive (MEDIUM).

main.text: When they buy tools, devices, or consumables for self-improvement, they are motivated by long-term adherence rather than novelty. Products are evaluated as routine infrastructure: if something creates recurring friction, it threatens consistency and therefore the promised benefit. They choose options that make the ‘good habit’ the easy default under real-life constraints.
main.rationalization: The window’s emphasis on livability benchmarks and ‘stop shopping once routine-safe’ aligns with this infrastructure framing (MEDIUM continuity).

implicit_goals.text: When building out household routines, they want a small set of products they can rely on daily without second-guessing accuracy, safety, or maintenance overhead. The goal isn’t maximal novelty; it’s a stable, low-friction system that still works when life is busy. They treat predictable behavior, persistence, and practical completeness as prerequisites for any claimed benefit.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Window evidence continued to tie product success to routine survivability—low friction, state retention, and completeness determine whether something becomes infrastructure (MEDIUM-HIGH).

implicit_goals.text: When they review a purchase, they want to be the person who pressure-tests practical pitfalls and publishes what other buyers usually learn after checkout. They aim to translate baseline comparisons, livability benchmarks, and transparency audits (what’s included, warranty/returns) into actionable guidance: what to expect, what to disable, and what would trigger a return. Their authority is built on repeatable real-world checks and explicit disclosures, not brand loyalty.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap evidence reinforced that their authority move is practical verification plus completeness and policy auditing, not generalized advice (HIGH, multiple window gaps).

implicit_goals.text: When choosing between ‘better on paper’ and ‘easier in life,’ they want improvement without turning tools into chores. Progress is defined as outcomes plus adherence: a product that is theoretically superior but practically annoying is not an upgrade. This makes them sensitive to small usability costs that accumulate and to whether a purchase can be a closed decision rather than an ongoing research project.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gaps continued to show them treating annoyance costs, completeness, and stop-shopping closure as decisive because they predict abandonment and wasted spend (MEDIUM-HIGH).

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: A Blood Pressure Sensor by Antrolife. for The Upper arm, it boasts The Biggest Backlit Display with The Most Accurate Adjustable and Pulse Rate Monitor. Bag, and USB-C
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
[[VIDEOID:dcb83b99b76f4e40016817be9850eac9]] I'm a guy who typically tries to set things up without reading the instructions and found that I had to read the instructions here to figure out how to set it up as the date/time settings were not intuitive to me. Set up was a bit of a pain and I used the single sheet instructions that were printed in LARGE type to do so, but I found that these instructions skipped some setting and the instruction booklet requires me to grab my reading glasses so I haven't sorted that out yet.

Once setup, it is easy to use WITHOUT THE VOICE and the readings are accurate as compared to an OMRON machine my wife uses. That said, this does take a bit longer than the OMRON.

The display is clear and easy to read and stores 99 readings for each of 2 users. It is easy to switch between users.

The device comes with a set of 4 AA batteries but also includes a USB A to C cable that can power the device. I was surprised that when I disconnected the USB cable to move the device from one room to another that my settings were saved so I did not have to go through reprogramming the date and time.

The verbal reading is helpful but with a thick accent. When I turn it off, I'm honestly not sure what the machine is saying - I think "measure over" but I'm not certain. I also find it odd that it reads out systolic and diastolic measurements as "X millimeter or MERcury" - I think just a number or even 106 millimeters but hearing "of MERcury" twice each reading will wear on me. There was some setting for PA which I turned off that gave a reading in some other measurement as well, but that was not useful to me.

Overall, this is easy to use (after setup) and does the job fine. The package includes the device, armband, batteries, a warranty card (12 months), USB cable, and a small carrying case.

I'm happy to recommend this Antrolife blood pressure monitor - it is easy to use, functions properly and the visual and verbal readout are easy to see/hear.
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.best_prediction_review:
I bought this Antrolife upper-arm BP monitor to keep a closer eye on my numbers at home (I’m pretty health-focused and like to track trends), and so far it’s been a practical unit that actually gets used.

Setup was basically: wrap cuff, press start, wait. The big backlit display is the main win for me — I can read it at arm’s length in a dim room without hunting for reading glasses. It finishes a reading in about the time it takes to brush my teeth, and the pump noise is in that same “bathroom appliance” range, not something that wakes the house.

Accuracy-wise, I compared several back-to-back readings against an older Omron I already trust, and it was usually very close (a few points off at most when I sat still and used the same arm position). That gave me enough confidence to use it for day-to-day monitoring rather than treating it like a random gadget.

Comfort is good: the cuff inflation feels firm but not painfully aggressive, and it deflates quickly so you’re not stuck clamped down. I also appreciate that it remembers settings/doesn’t make me reconfigure things every time I power it up.

Power has been easy — I like that it can run off USB-C, and it also came with what I needed to get started. The carry bag makes it simple to toss in a travel kit.

One note: if you’re sensitive to “extra” features, the voice readout is more annoyance than help (a bit repetitive), but it’s easy to turn off and then it’s just a straightforward monitor.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: Nose and Ear Hair Trimmer,Dailytop Painless Electric Nose/Ear Hair Removal for Men and Women,Battery-operated,Dual Edged Blade with Cleaning Brush
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
I've hit the point in my mid-50s that I need a trimmer like this.

Although this is the only nose trimer I've ever used, I can't see the need to consider another one.

The trimer performs well, and includes a battery (easy to find AA) and extra "blade".

Use is intuitive and easy. The machine is very quiet, the noise is less than my toothbrush. It is easily small enough to put in a bathroom travel kit.

The company offers a 12-month warranty and a 30 day return period.
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.best_prediction_review:
Bought this to keep nose/ear hair in check without risking scissors. It trims cleanly and fast (maybe 20–30 seconds total for both), with no pulling for me. Noise is about like an electric toothbrush—noticeable but not obnoxious—and it’s small enough to toss in a travel kit.

It’s basically one-button simple, even half-awake. My only ding: mine didn’t include a AA battery, so plan on supplying one. Cleaning is easy: pop the head off and use the little brush, then a quick rinse and air-dry. So far it feels like decent value for a basic trimmer.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: MEO Lite Reusable and Adjustable Face Mask | 2 Replaceable Filters | Washable, Fashionable, Sustainable, Comfortable | x 1 Medium, Black
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
I've gone through a lot of masks even though I try to stay at home. I've still got too many medical/dental/lab test appointments and "emergency" food runs. Although most of my food shopping is delivery or curbside pickup, I still wear a mask for pickup.

This is a well made made. The ear loops are adjustable and stay on reliably. The filters are good quality (I tested them when it was cold enough to see your breath, and there was a significant difference when the filter was in the mask) and it is comfortable. Although I double mask (this over a KN95) I do not have any issue breathing with this and another mask on.

I'm very satisfied with this mask and would recommend it.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: Sitz Bath for Toilet Seat, Postpartum Care, Hemorrhoid Treatment, Bidet, Yoni Steam Seat, Collapsible Save Space Universal fit - Oval - Elongated
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
My doctor recommended that I use a sitz bath regularly following a medical issue, and I've used the "standard" hard plastic one I purchased at my local medical supply store. The problem I had with that one is that it simply does not fit on an elongated seat, meaning that I'm relegated to one of the many bathrooms in the house to use it.

I saw this listing and decided to give it a try since the page indicated it would work on either type of toilet seat. Not only does it fit on both the elongated and round toilets, but also fits fine on the elongated toilets on which I have installed large bidet attachments!

The sitz bath itself is very nice - it is softer plastic than the one I'd been using. At first, I didn't get the collapsible portion of the bath as I couldn't figure out what purpose it serves. Now that I've used it for a while I remain confused about WHY this is needed - it changes the depth a bit, so it might be easier to fit in a suitcase, but who's traveling with a sitz bath? I guess maybe it makes storage a bit easier, but since mine is used on a daily basis, I'm not collapsing it to store.

The bath itself is a bit wider than the one it is replacing and, thanks to the collapsible portion, a bit deeper as well. Overall, this is a simple product that functions well. I am happy with it.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: Comfortable Spine Deck Back Stretcher Set – Durable Upper & Lower Back Stretcher for Pain Relief – Adjustable Spine Board – Orthopedic Lumbar Back Stretching Device + Bag + Massage Roller + Strap
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
This is a rather simple back stretcher that includes the stretcher, a thin nylon carry bag, a belt to attach to a chair, and a ball. While there are minimalist instructions, I found they were not sufficient for me to figure out exactly how to use this (that is, where on my back to put the curve when I was lying down.

It is a simple yet sturdy device. It does require some strength to move the positions of the stretcher and for the initial install. If this is for an elderly or weak person, assistance may be needed.

I've done the stretches (2x a day, 5 minutes at a time) for 5 days and I definitely feel the stretch. It is easier to do now as compared to a few days ago, but I'm not ready yet to move to a deeper stretch.

If the instructions were better and did not require my own research, this would be a five star review.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: 3 Pairs Small Reading Glasses Men and Women,Pen Clip Portable Metal Case +2.5
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
These are very compact and great to have in my pocket when I go shopping, but at home I've got better reading glasses to use.

The three-pack comes with the glasses in nice cases that close well. The package also includes three cleaning cloths.

The glasses are VERY small and, at least for me, best use for short time periods. I use them primarily while shopping as they are helpful for reading ingredients and I keep a pair in the kitchen to read labels as well. If I'm going to wear reading glasses to read a book or newspaper, I feel more comfortable with larger glasses that do a better job of staying on - these have short arms and don't do the best job of staying on if I am moving about.

Overall, they function pretty well, but for short term use.

I have learned that not all magnesium supplements are created equally and that some forms of magnesium are more readily absorbed than other forms - the National Institute of Health notes "studies have found that magnesium in the aspartate, citrate, lactate, and chloride forms is absorbed more completely and is more bioavailable than magnesium oxide and magnesium sulfate." As a result, I switched from the less expensive magnesium oxide I had been taking to using more absorbable forms of magnesium. My research led me to find that magnesium diglycinate is one of the most readily available forms of magnesium for digestion. One study, entitled Bioavailability of Magnesium Diglycinate vs Magnesium Oxide in Patients with Ileal Resection confirms how much better-absorbed magnesium diglycinate is over the common magnesium oxide. As a result, magnesium diglycinate is one of the best magnesium forms available for absorption.

For a magnesium supplement, this has almost all the positives going for it (the only thing lacking is they are not organic):

Made in the USA
Third-party tested
Vegan capsule
Uses Magnesium Diglycinate
Well-sized and easy to swallow

The only issue I have with this supplement is that to get 420 mg of magnesium requires 3 capsules rather than the daily dosage indicated on the package of 2 capsules. Given the emphasis on the product page and package of 420 mg of magnesium and that the product contains 60 servings, I believed that one serving contained 420 mg of magnesium when the 2 capsule recommended serving contains only 280 mg of magnesium.

AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.product_description: Plus Vitae Apple Cider Vinegar Gummies with Ginger Extract - Organic ACV Gummies - Fat Loss & Cleanse - Pure Support Gummy from"The Mother" - 60 Count
AH5TSSJLJDBXZBDB2ID6Q45H627Q.review_text:
I ordered this package of apple cider vinegar gummies understanding that the product was organic, but it does not appear that anything in the ingredients list s organic. I also note that the page shows the ingredients to be "Ginger(root), Cane Sugar, Glucose Syrup, Water, Citrus Pectin, Citric Acid, Sodium Citrate, Natural Flavors" whereas the actual packaging shows the ingredients to be "Cane Sugar, Glucose Syrup (Corn Syrup), Water, Citrus Pectin, Citric Acid, Sodium Citrate, Adipic Acid, Natural Apple Flavor, Black Carrot Extract, Curcumin, Spirulina Extract for Color, Maple Syrup, Chili Pepper." That's a LOT of ingredients on the package that aren't on the list provided on the product page and it seems, given that ingredients lists are typically in the order of prominence, that the most prominent ingredient, per the product page list, is ginger, which it clearly is not as each serving contains 2 mg of ginger but 5 grams of added sugars.

I can't find any reference to anything in here that is organic, other than the listing on the product page proclaiming "Organic AVC Gummies". As someone who typically buys organic whenever given the choice, I'm used to seeing (a) an ingredients list that shows what is organic and (b) a license logo from whoever is certifying the product or its ingredients are organic; both are lacking here.

The fact that the ingredients list on the product page and on the package do not match is of significant concern to me.

As to the product itself, the odor of vinegar is much more prominent here than with other AVC gummies that I've used. While the heavy sugar keeps the vinegar from being too strong, the taste is still there and the gummies themselves are much more grainy than any of the other AVC gummies I have used. While other gummies with the same amount of sugar seem to be more candy-like to me, these are not as easy to chew and swallow.

I'm also confused by the need to take 2 gummies to get one serving; the amount of sugar in each serving is 5 grams and the amount of ACV is 10% of that, meaning if they simply cut the sugar by 10%, the full amount of AVC could get ONE gummy. Perhaps they need the two gummies to split up the vinegar flavor, but the amount of sugar strikes me as very high.

After the issue with the ingredients listing, that is that what is on the package differs materially from the product listing, I have little faith in this product.

AHHRSKCGTLKOUOWAHLZHSERSX4UA.product_description: Plant JYM, 24g Premium Plant Based Protein Powder, Dairy Free, Vegan Friendly, 5g BCAA, Low Carb, Keto Friendly, Active Men & Woman, Advanced Recovery, by JYM Supplement Science, Oatmeal Cookie, 2lbs

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: WHY MAGNESIUM BISGLYCINATE CHELATE ADVANCED COMPLEX IS BETTER AND MORE BIOAVAILABLE? OUR COMMITMENT TO EXCEPTIONAL QUALITY. We create the perfect balance and superior supplements through the latest technologies and the purest nutrients nature offers us. Our compromise is to improve the health of those that trust our superior quality supplements to enhance their lives. Integrity and quality steer everything we make, and our commitment to our customers is to produce the highest advanced quality nutritional supplements out there. MAGNESIUM BISGLYCINATE ADVANCED COMPLEX supports more than 300 biochemical reactions in your body, such as healthy muscle and nerve function, calmness, improved sleep, exercise recovery, energy boost, cramps, bone, and joint strength, cognitive function, cardiovascular health, cellular energy production, and relaxation. Magnesium Is Safe and an essential mineral for good health that supports hundreds of chemical reactions in your body. However, many people get less than they need.
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Magnesium is an important mineral, and many people are deficient in it. For this reason, magnesium can help with heart issues and even help with sleep. Some people even get relief from headaches or anxiety from taking it.

Magnesium comes in a variety of forms, and some are better absorbed and utilized by the body than others. Magnesium glycinate is one of the best forms, and the body seems to absorb more magnesium from this than other, cheaper forms (like magnesium oxide).

I have taken magnesium for many years and am healthier for it. I usually take it before bed as part of my nighttime supplements. The company seems to be quality and suggests that they are a supplier and as such, do their own testing and tracking.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Momentous Huberman Stack, Fadogia Agrestis, 60 Servings
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Fadogia has been shown to raise testosterone levels significantly (at least in rat studies) and has been recommended by many for this purpose in humans. So, it likely will work in this regard, at least to a degree.

However, another study on rats found that fadogia in amounts similar to what is available in supplements caused damage in the testes, liver, and kidneys of rats, and there were signs of cell damage, including in lower doses that are more common in human supplement amounts.

I believe in supplements and take a lot of them, but I am also very careful with what I put in my body. The research on this herb is emerging and it is important to be very careful. I recommend caution. If you use it, I suggest a low dose (no more than one pill) and alternating use on-and-off.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Guerrilla Nine - BCAA Amino Acids - Post Workout Recovery Drink for Men and Women - 30 Servings of Strawberry Lemonade Flavor - Muscle Recovery Through Amino9 an Essential Amino Acids EAA Powder
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I like taking BCAAs and EAAs after a workout, to provide some protein replenishment after working out. Plus I like drinking something flavorful to hydrate myself.

This is an interesting formula. Not only does it contain essential amino acids, but it adds ashwagandha, and I'm not sure of the reason for that. Ashwagandha has a relaxing impact, but also may have a positive impact on testosterone, so I am not sure of the point of it.

Given my preferences for a post-workout, I add taurine powder to this.

The flavor is pretty decent, but I have very few standards with workout formulas, so I may not be the best person to ask on these things.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Alpha GPC Choline 600mg + Uridine Monophosphate 300mg -2-in-1 Nootropic Supplement Helps Boost Focus, Energy & Cognitive Performance -Potent Mood Enhancer & Brain Focus Supplements -60 Veggie Capsules
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This formula contains two nootropic agents, i.e. chemicals that might have cognitive benefits. Both of them give me vivid dreams when taken at night, and I notice a subtle mental lift from both of them. Alpha-GPC is a form of choline that allows for the production of acetylcholine in the brain (be aware that too much acetylcholine can potentially cause depressive thoughts, so don't overdo it).

When taking this product, I do notice that lift. However, I am not sure if it's real or just a placebo effect.

Why? The price on this is really good for these two chemicals, since both are rather expensive. That concerns me a little, since if it's too good to be true, it might be the case! I will give them the benefit of the doubt, especially since the label says there is "third party testing." So if you want these two chemicals for a good price, give this a try.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Pre Workout Demon (Berry Flavour) - Hardcore Pre-Workout Supplement with Creatine, Caffeine, Beta-Alanine and Glutamine, 40 Servings
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
When I get a pre-workout, I like it to have creatine, beta-alanine, and caffeine. These are three elements that make me feel "ready to go" in terms of working out. The addition of glutamine, taurine, and citrulline is also cool, since I often put those in my "mid-workout" which I take partway through the workout when I do cardio and lift.

So this formula pretty much takes care of everything I need. The only thing I noticed is that the caffeine content is pretty high, and since I'm a big coffee drinker, it's just worth noting that your daily caffeine content can really shoot up if you combine this with coffee and aren't sensitized.

The taste is pretty good too. I look forward to it.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: KORESELECT Wellness - Blood Circulation Supplements, 100% Vegan, Korean Red Panax Ginseng Extract, Dietary Herbal Supplement for Men & Women, Stress Relief, Immune System Support - 45 Softgels Pills
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Ginseng may have a variety of benefits, including providing a feeling of increased energy and perhaps even helping with certain "male issues." I have taken it off and on since high school, and it seems to make me feel better when I took it even back then.

Ginseng is often rather expensive, and there are other ways to get many of the benefits of ginseng that are much cheaper options. However, ginseng is nonetheless a very renowned herb and known (at least in traditional medicine) to have a lot of supposed benefits.

This brand uses mature ginseng (so it has more time to develop nutrients) and also does extensive testing, which is always a good thing when considering which brand to go with.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Inno Supps Natural Vegan BCAAs + EAA
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a good post-workout (or, for me, often a mid-workout formula). It contains branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) that are vegan, which isn't a big concern for me, but if you are concerned about that, then that's a plus. It also contains glutamine, and all the essential amino acids. Adding EAAs not only has been shown to help build muscle mass, but can help vegans get a more balanced amino acid profile.

The flavor is really good too! Even though I'm not vegan, this is a formula that anyone can appreciate and use.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: PUREMEDICS NAC 600 mg (N-Acetylcysteine 600mg) - NAC Supplement to Support Glutathione Formation and Respiratory Function - Doctor Formulated - 3rd Party Certified - Hypoallergenic - 250 Capsules
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
N-acetyl cysteine has some potential lung and liver health benefits, and I have taken it fairly regularly for almost 10 years. Since it may raise glutathione levels in the body, it could potentially reverse the graying of hair as well, at least in theory.

NAC is best taken on an empty stomach, since it is an amino acid. And, I suggest not taking it before exercise, since it has antioxidant activity, and too much antioxidant activity during exercise can actually negate the benefits of exercise (you want some bodily stress to happen).

My main issue with this is the price. It's more expensive (as of now at least) than most other brands of NAC I have ever bought.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Nature’s Edge Q-Gel 100 Dietary Supplement, 60 softgels
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Co-Enzyme Q10 is an anti-aging nutrient I have taken for many years. It has been shown to help with heart health issues and other health concerns.

This formula contains co-enzyme Q10 paired with lecithin, which has been shown to help its absorption and utilization by the body. As such, it is a better way to get co-enzyme Q10, which can be difficult for the body to absorb and utilize. Note that this is not the ubiquinol form of Q10, which is ultimately the best-absorbed, but which is also very expensive.

So far, I have been very satisfied with this brand. I recommend this if you want a good form of Q10.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Vitamin D, K2, K1, Zinc, Quercetin, Elderberry, Turmeric, Ginger & Echinacea Whole Food Supplement 8 in 1- D3 5000 IU Plant-Based from Lichen, Vitamin K (K2 MK7, MK4, K1), D3 K2 Vitamins - 60Capsules
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a good formula that combines Vitamin D3 with multiple forms of Vitamin K.

Typically, Vitamin D and K2 are paired together, since both are important for bone health and calcium regulation in the body (to make sure calcium ends up in the bones and not inside your arteries).

However, this formula also contains K1, which helps with blood clotting. So, if you are on blood thinners, K1 might impact this. Nonetheless, K1 is still an essential nutrient and is present in green, leafy vegetables, so the amount here will not impact those who aren't on blood thinners as a life-saving necessity.

Be sure to take this pill with some type of fat or with a meal since both vitamins are fat-soluble. Also, this formula contains black pepper extract, which may increase the absorption of both vitamins. Be aware that black pepper extract can interact with certain drugs.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Opticaps Advanced Vision Formula Softgels with Lutein & Zeaxanthin Plus Bilberry from Basic Vigor
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a decent eye health supplement that is high in lutein, and reasonable in its zeaxanthin content (typically there is a lot less zeaxanthin than lutein when they are paired together in nature).

Both carotenoids are present in the eye and supplementing with lutein can help with overall eye health and possibly prevent certain eye diseases. There is a very little bit of bilberry extract in this, but not enough to do much.

The price is a little high for a lutein supplement, but it's not too bad. Overall, this is a good eye health formula. I recommend it, especially if you don't eat a lot of foods high in lutein (like kale).

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Mushroom Supplement with Lions Mane, Reishi, Chaga, Maitake - Daily Immune Support and Nootropic Brain Support Formula - Made in USA - Natural Energy and Focus - 60 Capsules
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a good mushroom blend that contains extracts of the fruiting body of various medicinal mushrooms. Lion's mane in particular may have nootropic benefits, and I have taken it at various times for that benefit.

That the company claims to third-party test is good because a lot of mushroom supplements don't contain a lot of the fruiting body and therefore are pretty worthless.

Finding information about this company was challenging since apparently "Nature's Nutrition" is the name of multiple companies. Their website is pretty boilerplate without going into many details about them, or how they test.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Kate Naturals Myo-Inositol & D-Chiro Inositol Blend (60 Capsules). Fertility Supplements for Women. Hormonal Balance & Healthy Ovarian Function Support for Women. Optimal 40:1 Ratio. Vitamin B8
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Inositol is a sugar alcohol once thought to be a B vitamin (it isn't actually a vitamin), but it does seem to have many benefits in the body. When I take it before bed, it really does help with sleep, and seems to also positively impact serotonin levels even into the next day.

It also may help with female fertility issues, and this product contains a ratio of two different types of inositol based on some studies related to that issue.

I took off a star, because while the description here does explain how to open the bottle lid and close it to keep the product fresh (I'm glad they made that clear), it doesn't mention anything about third-party testing, which I like to see when buying supplements. It does provide me with the sleep benefits of inositol, so it likely is pure.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Nature’s Edge® QGel® 100mg A Superior Form of Coenzyme Q10-30 Counts
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Co-enzyme Q10 has been shown to have multiple benefits, and I have taken it for many years as a general anti-aging supplement. This supplement is labeled ubidecarenone, which is basically a synonym for ubiquinone, the more common name for co-enzyme Q10. I admit I have never seen that chemical name used, so I had to look it up.

This is co-enzyme Q10 that is supposed to be absorbed better. I'm guessing this is because it is mixed with lecithin, which has been shown to enhance the absorption of co-enzyme Q10 and a variety of other chemicals. Since co-enzyme Q10 can often be challenging to absorb, this seems like a good formula.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Activated Probiotic – Etana Beauty – 30 Day Supply – 8 Billion CFU Probiotic + Prebiotic FOS & Acacia Fiber + Freeze–Dried Supernatant Culture
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a good probiotic formula that contains probiotics (good bacteria), prebiotics (food for the good bacteria), and post-biotics (products produced by good bacteria). This makes this formula kind of unique, in that the inclusion of post-biotics is a new thing. To some degree, this may be a novelty, but there is some research that shows that for people who really can't tolerate probiotics, post-biotics may have benefits.

This is a solid formula in this regard, although the number of units (4 billion per pill) is a little low compared to other formulas, some of which go into the 10-50 billion. However, this range of CFUs is about right for the price.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Trusted Nutrients Chlorella Broken Cell Wall Supplement | Green Superfood | Supports Gut Health | Rich Antioxidant Supplement | 600 mg 60 Vegetarian Capsules
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Chlorella is an algae "super-food" and rich in all kinds of vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients. If you are looking for something "green" to take for overall health, this is a great option.

This brand seems good and the price is pretty reasonable for pills. It is much cheaper if you buy it in bulk and mix it with water, but that also tastes pretty bad. So, if you need chlorella pills, I recommend this.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Organifi: Move Organic Joint Support - 60 Softgels - Supports Overall Joint Health and Immunity - with Superfoods Arctic Pine Bark, Astaxanthin, Holy Basil and Turmeric - No Gluten, Soy or Dairy
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a powerful formula for joint pain or other inflammatory problems. It contains a variety of ingredients that have been shown to positively impact joint health.

I like to take certain supplements to preventatively address joint issues, since at age 41, I work out and lift weights almost daily, and run anywhere from 70-100 miles a month in warmer weather. So far, I have avoided any joint issues, thanks in part to supplements like glucosamine sulfate, MSM, and some of the ones in this formula.

I like that it contains a form of turmeric that is readily absorbed and utilized. If you look up the research behind Turmipure gold, you'll see that it is absorbed a lot better compared to regular turmeric extract that is 95% Curcumin. I am guessing this is because it is complexed with lecithin, which should also help the other ingredients absorb better.

The inclusion of astaxanthin is good as well, and I take it regularly as an overall anti-aging supplement, as well as for skin health.

The main drawback I see is the price. It is pretty high, even if you were to buy the various ingredients separately.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Staunch Tudca 250MG, 60 Capsules - Non-GMO
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Tudca, aka Tauroursodeoxycholic acid, is basically a water-soluble bile salt, which means it emulsifies fats (breaks them down into smaller units so lipase can work on them more effectively). So, it can help with fat digestion, especially for those with gall bladder issues, but it also has many other benefits.

Where Tudca shines is that because it is water-soluble, it can clean out the bile salts that can get backed up in the liver, which causes liver problems. It has also been shown to have anti-aging properties because it apparently can protect the endoplasmic reticulum of all cells, and thus have positive implications in preventing diabetes and even neurological diseases.

While I know that Staunch Labs produces workout-related supplements, the evidence that Tudca can help with muscle building is very slim, but some bodybuilders swear by it. I personally am not sure.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Omax Daily Kids Probiotic and Prebiotic | Berry Blast Chewable for Digestive Support and Immune Support | 5 Billion CFU | 5 Strains, Vegan Smart, Gluten Free | 30 Count
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a good probiotic for kids. It tastes good and what's important for me is that it includes a hefty dose of probiotics, at least for a chewable tablet: 5 billion CFU.

The formula also includes inulin, which is a good prebiotic, i.e. food for the good bacteria. I look for this when buying a probiotic.

For my 8-year old daughter, the real issue is taste, and she really does like the taste of this, so this product is a winner by my definition.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Body Vigor Joint Ready, Glucosamine Sulfate, MSM, Turmeric, Ginger, and Boswellia, Joint Health Supplement, 90 Tablets
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I have been a very active person since high school, and took up regularly running and working out when I was in my early 20s in the early 2000s. Since then, I have consistently run and lifted. Right now, I pretty much use the Stairmaster 4-5 times a week (burning about 700-1000 calories each time) and lifting 3 times a week. In the summer months, I run 50-75 miles a month.

I'm not saying this to brag, but to mention that I put my body through a lot, and at 41 I have no major (or minor) joint issue. I attribute this to a variety of things, including taking glucosamine and other supplements since my early 20s.

This formula is high in glucosamine sulfate (the sulfate form is more expensive but superior to glucosamine HCL) and also includes quite a few other major anti-inflammatory herbs and chemicals. It has standardized boswellia and turmeric, which will help with inflammation issues. I take both of these regularly.

The price is kind of high, but there are a lot of expensive ingredients in here.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Intestinal Fortitude - Pro-B11 - Probiotic Supplement - 60 Capsules - 11 Bacterial Strains Including 5 Lactobacillus and 5 Bifidobacterium, Support Gastrointestinal Health, Create A Healthy Microbiome
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I regularly take probiotics because of a lot of promising research that is coming out related to gut bacteria having a lot of benefits, digestive and even beyond.

This seems to be a really good product, with 11 billion CFUs of probiotics, and a guaranteed 4 billion by the expiration date. It has multiple strains and is timed release, probably to release them into the gut and avoid destruction in the stomach.

This "11 billion" amount was kind of confusing for me at first, since the label says "4 billion." However, if you look at the bottom of the back side, below the label, it explains it has 11 billion at the time of manufacture. It is kind of cool that they guarantee 4 billion until expiration. Most companies aren't that transparent.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Evogen Greentein | Pure Grain Free Plant Protein, Pea, Pumpkin, Watermelon Seed Proteins Vitamin B12, Dairy Free, Gluten Free, for Recovery and Shakes (Dark Chocolate Hazelnut)
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
This is a decent plant-protein formula that tastes great and is fairly low in carbs, which is the main thing I look for in a protein powder, since I am on a low carb diet and stick to it.

I just mix it with a few tablespoons of half-and-half (yes I know this makes it non-vegan, so this could easily be duplicated by using some plant-based milk) and create a pudding-like mix. It is a high-protein, lower carb dessert for me.

My main issue with this is that I don't like consuming sugar if I don't have to, and from what I can tell this is sweetened with brown rice syrup and maltodextrin. While, yes, brown rice sugar is more natural than refined sugar, and it isn't high in fructose, it is still basically a fancy source of sugar. Overall, however, this is a good powder that is still relatively low in carbs.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: 1Mission Nutrition, Pre Workout Powder, Fat Burning Pre-Workout, Great for Strength, Energy and Pumps (30 Servings, Watermelon Rage)
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I use pre-workout formula before most workouts. I tend to look for a few things in a pre-workout, namely creatine, beta-alanine, and caffeine. These chemicals not only help me feel mental and physical energy, but also seem to have performance benefits. This formula contains all of these supplements.

It also has some other beneficial chemicals that I don't necessarily need in a pre-workout, but I like. These include betaine and taurine. I usually add taurine in my post-workout, but having it in a pre-workout is fine as well.

Some of the other ingredients may have various mental and physical benefits during a workout, including DMAE which can give me a mental lift.

So, I really like this pre-workout, and I have to say that it tastes pretty good. I recommend it!

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Astaxanthin 10mg - 100% Pure Antioxidant Supplement, Boosting Energy & Strength, Helps Skin Care & Eye, Arthritic Joints, Healthy Aging, 60 pcs - USA Made
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I have taken astaxanthin for a few years, as an overall health supplement, but also specifically for skin health. It is indeed a powerful supplement, and one of the most potent carotenoids. It is derived from a type of algae, and is what makes both salmon and pink flamingos have their characteristic pink color.

This seems like good astaxanthin. The only reason I took off a star is I can't seem to find much about this company online. I like to check up on supplement companies to see what they are about in terms of history and quality control.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Potent Inositol 2050mg Supplement | 40:1 Ratio Myo-Inositol & D-Chiro Inositol with Folic Acid For Female Support | Hormonal Balance & Healthy Ovarian Function| Vitamin B8 & B9 | 30 day Supply
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Inositol is a sugar-alcohol and was once thought to be vitamin B8. However, it isn't actually a vitamin, but it is beneficial to the body, and is found along with choline in lecithin.

Inositol can help with sleep, mood, and may even with female fertility. This formula is of higher strength than others, but the pills are pretty big. It also includes some folic acid.

I take inositol before bed each night and it does seem to help me sleep longer, and stops me from waking up so much in the middle of the night.

I am taking off a star because I can't find references to third-party testing and the verbiage on their website is filled with spelling mistakes and grammar issues. While this doesn't necessarily indicate problems with the supplement, I like to see a company that seem fully reputable.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: Black Seed Oil Organic Cumin-Capsules Black Cumin Seed Oil, Cold Pressed, Nigella Sativa Extract, Kalonji Oil, Black Seed, Hair&Skin Omegas - 60CT Black Seed Oil Capsules, Herbal Goodness, USA Made
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Black cumin seed oil has a variety of benefits, and I have taken it for a little over a year. After taking it, I noticed a lot more drive and mental energy to get things done. This has been the best benefit of black seed oil for me. I take it regularly for a few weeks, and then take a few days off to make sure the effect doesn't go away.

This is an extract, which is standardized for the active ingredient, which explains why it is more expensive than other pills. It will, however, also be more effective than plain old black cumin seed oil, which doesn't have a set amount of the active ingredient guaranteed.

This is a good product, and the price is pretty good for the extract.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: BrainiLex | Clinically Tested Brain Supplement | Memory, Focus, Learn, Perform | 90 Count
AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
Based on some research, this supplement may work, over time, to increase mental stamina and clarity. The main ingredient is an extract of the cocoons of a type of moth! I am not going to lie, that is kind of wild, and I have taken almost every supplement under the sun. There is, however, some evidence it works. I personally haven't felt anything, but then again, I am already taking quite a few brain-enhancing supplements and my memory is already very sharp.

However, the price is really high, probably because we are dealing with something as time-consuming as collecting moth cocoons to get the main ingredient. If you want to give it a go, it may work, but the price is prohibitive in my opinion. Nonetheless, it's a pretty fascinating supplement.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.review_text:
I take taurine as part of my post-workout formula, in part because it helps balance out taking beta-alanine, which may deplete taurine (and beta-alanine is in my pre-workout).

But Taurine has its own benefits, including potentially protecting the heart, brain, and muscles. I also feel a good mental lift from taking taurine, and there is some evidence it can enhance athletic performance, which is why it is an ingredient in some pre-workout formulas.

I normally use taurine powder, but if you would prefer pills, this is a good option. However, in pill form, it ends up being much more expensive, gram for gram, than powder.

AGFHZOVGXHPFTP23A2ZWMQ72ODLQ.product_description: LiftMode Icariin Powder Supplement - Horny Goat Weed Extract Supplement Supports Energy, Vitality & Stamina, Epimedium - Vegetarian, Vegan, Non-GMO, Gluten Free - 10 Grams (200 Servings)

AGNURT6BJA6DDBXSIIRHPN5AG5CA.product_description: BIOHM Super Reds Superfood Powder - 30 Servings - Probiotics, Super Beets, Tart Cherry Extract, and 17 Red Whole Foods Packed with Antioxidants, Allergen Free, Non-GMO, Vegetarian

Maybe their flavor is overwhelmed by pomegranate, cherry, blueberry, cranberry, raspberry, and strawberry? Maybe it is the mushrooms, seeds, or whatever. No, the ingredients are all good, EXCEPT:

Why aren't all those good ingredients certified organic? This is for a health drink, right?

Most of the ingredients contain natural sugars, so why add the monk fruit? I'm glad it isn't stevia, which gives me immediate indigestion. However, why add monk fruit? Yes, they're doing it to make the powder sweet. Why treat their mostly adult purchasers like children who will only eat fruits and vegetables that are sweetened?

Use organic ingredients and lose the monk fruit. Do that and I'll be back as a customer.

s natural sugars and further sweetened with monk fruit extract.

My only gripe is that it already has 7 grams of sugar per scoop - personally, I don't understand why the manufacturer felt the necessity of adding the monk fruit (which doesn't really add much to sugar content,) to an already naturally sweet formula. AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.product_description: NATALIST Omega DHA Fish Oil Fertility & Pregnancy Support Supplement Pure Marine Lipids Triglyceride EPA & DHA Formula for Women Enteric-Coated No Fishy Aftertaste Gluten-Free - 60 Softgels
AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.review_text:
Fish oil is a supplement where quality matters since a lot of fish oils contain mercury or less than the advertised ingredients. You should look for fish oil that is IFOS-certified and pay extra for that. A LabDoor rating also helps. The source location and supplier of the fish oil are not disclosed.

The current cost is $30, or $0.50 per serving. InnovixLabs fish oil, which is IFOS certified and has a high LabDoor score, costs $0.14 for a roughly equivalent serving.

AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.product_description: True Grace Omega-3 Fish Oil - 60 Softgels - 1400 mg EPA + DHA - Brain, Heart, Joint & Immune Health - Organic, Gluten Free, Soy Free
AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.review_text:
The front of the bottle says it has oil from wild Pollock and the back says it contains Tilapia, which is generally an unhealthy farm-raised fish that should be avoided. If the tilapia is only used to make the gelatin, the label should mention that. Oil from fish at the low end of the food chain would be better since those fish are less contaminated. Anchovies and sardines would be better. This fish oil does not have independent quality verifications listed, which is important for fish oil. This lacks IFOS certification or certification from LabDoor. If this is a natural triglyceride form of the oil, that means it is more likely to have contaminants. If this is synthetic triglyceride form, they should mention that. Other products that use AlaskOmega oil are IFOS certified, so there is a chance that this oil would pass those same tests, but the company should advertise that more.

They added lemon flavor to the oil inside the capsules, which is unusual. It might be done to mask the taste of low-quality fish oil from people that open the capsules to evaluate freshness.

The bottle is nice. The current price is $64 for 90 servings, or $0.71/serving. Doctor’s Best is selling very similar AlaskOmega Ethyl Ester oil for $0.43/serving. SmarterVitamins is also selling a nearly identical AlaskOmega product for less money.

AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.product_description: Alpha Evolve, Natural Testosterone Support - Hormone Support- Powerful Estrogen Blocker - 180 Veggie Capsules , 1 Month Supply
AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.review_text:
This product has ingredients that support natural testosterone production and increase libido. The effects are noticeable, although I doubt my actual testosterone levels are increased significantly. The noticeable effects are more likely due to increased dopamine.

Some of these ingredients you need to be careful of, specifically the L-Dopa and DHEA. Too much of these supplements can cause anxiety and aggression. To my knowledge Tribulus does nothing and it is the dominant ingredient. The pills have a funky smell, which is from the indole-3-carbinol. I3C coverts to DIM in your body, and DIM is in these as well, which is redundant.

Another supplement that seems to work is D-Aspartic Acid, but this is a bulk powder not a pill.
None of these easily-available supplements will have a huge impact on your testosterone levels compared to more powerful compounds that are harder to buy, but they should help slightly.

The current price is $50, which is $0.83 per serving. You might only want to take one pill at a time instead of three.

AH4Z47YBJ7FV42RHZBLKLGHH35CA.review_text:
